segments.caption: The video opens with a dark space scene featuring a small bright object, likely a satellite or spacecraft, moving across the blackness of space, followed by a close-up of the Moon. The International Space Station (ISS) then appears, illuminated against the darkness, with its solar arrays glowing orange. The scene transitions to an interior shot of a man in a blue NASA flight suit with patches, including an American flag, speaking in what appears to be a spacecraft or control room. The video cuts to an aerial view of the ISS orbiting Earth, with clouds and oceans visible below. A brief shot of the Moon and Earth from space is shown, followed by a stunning night sky with the Milky Way and a silhouetted dead tree. The ISS is shown again from various angles, including a side view with Earth’s curvature and a top-down view over cloud-covered terrain. The man in the blue flight suit reappears, speaking again. The video then shows a woman with glasses, wearing a patterned top under a blue cardigan, speaking and gesturing with her hands in what looks like a technical or industrial setting. The ISS is shown once more, this time with a bright light source, possibly from a spacecraft or camera flash, illuminating part of the station. The final shots are of the ISS orbiting Earth, with clear views of the solar arrays and Earth’s surface below, including landmasses and oceans.

The camera pans around the station, highlighting its complex structure and the curvature of the planet below. A close-up reveals a detailed view of the station’s external components, including a robotic arm and docking ports, with Earth’s surface visible in the distance. The video then cuts to an interior shot of an astronaut in a blue flight suit, working with equipment in a cluttered module, followed by a view of a hatch leading to another section of the station. The exterior shots return, showing the station from various angles, including a dramatic view as it enters Earth’s shadow, with only the solar arrays faintly illuminated. The segment concludes with an interview shot of Danielle George, MBE, an electronics engineer from the University of Manchester, speaking in front of a modern, glass-walled building, with the International Space Station briefly superimposed over her image, linking her expertise to the space station’s technology.

The segment concludes with a wide-angle view of the ISS from space, highlighting its complex structure against the planet’s blue and white surface, with the National Geographic logo consistently displayed in the bottom right corner.

segments.caption: The video opens with a breathtaking view from the International Space Station (ISS), showing Earth’s curvature with swirling clouds and landmasses, framed by the station’s robotic arm and solar arrays. The ISS is shown again from space, rotating slowly with Earth below, followed by a wide shot of the full station orbiting above a blue and white planet. A woman, identified as Allison McIntyre, Chief of Space Vehicle Mockup Facility at NASA Johnson, speaks directly to the camera, with spacecraft mockups visible behind her. segments.caption: The video opens with a breathtaking view from inside the International Space Station, looking out through a window at Earth’s curved horizon under a bright blue sky with scattered clouds. The camera pans to reveal the station’s exterior, including the robotic arm Canadarm2 and solar arrays, as the station orbits above the planet. The scene transitions to a NASA Mission Control Center, where Leslie Ringo, identified as a CAPCOM Flight Controller, speaks directly to the camera, gesturing as she explains operations. Archival footage follows, showing a U.S. Air Force aircraft in flight, then a control room with operators monitoring systems, including a close-up of a control panel with switches labeled 'SLEW CONTROL' and 'MODE STATUS'. The video then cuts to a detailed exterior view of the ISS, highlighting its complex structure, solar panels, and antennas, with Earth in the background. A spacewalk is shown, with an astronaut in a white spacesuit working near the station’s truss, using Canadarm2 for support. The scene shifts to a satellite in orbit, followed by a ground-based radio telescope illuminated at night under a starry sky with the Milky Way visible. The video returns to Mission Control, showing a wide shot of the room with multiple operators at desks, large screens displaying orbital trajectories and telemetry, and a close-up of a control station labeled 'THOR'. The final frames show a close-up of a control panel with a 'JAPAN' sticker, and a technician in a white shirt and tie at a station with monitors displaying coordinates and system data, emphasizing the global collaboration in space operations.

segments.caption: The video opens with a large screen in a mission control room displaying multiple live feeds of the International Space Station (ISS) orbiting Earth, showing views of the station’s solar arrays, truss structure, and Earth’s curvature with auroras and city lights. The screen also features mission patches and telemetry data. The scene transitions to a control room where two women are seated at a console labeled 'MISSION OPERATIONS' with NASA and SpaceX logos, working on computers and monitoring the mission. The camera then cuts to a group of people, including a young woman in a blue jacket, reacting emotionally as they watch the event, some on phones. Next, astronauts in blue flight suits are shown inside the ISS, floating in microgravity, eating from pouches and interacting casually, with religious icons visible on the wall behind them. The video returns to the Mission Control Center, showing a large screen with a global map of the ISS’s orbital path, marked with ground station locations and telemetry data. A woman, identified as Leslie Ringo, CAPCOM Flight Controller, speaks directly to the camera, gesturing as she explains the mission. The final shot shows a Falcon 9 rocket on the launch pad, surrounded by support towers, as it ignites and begins its ascent, with smoke and fire billowing from the engines. Throughout the segment, the National Geographic logo is visible in the bottom right corner.

segments.caption: The video begins with a dramatic rocket launch, showing a Falcon 9 rocket igniting and ascending from a launch pad surrounded by tall support towers, with a plume of smoke and fire trailing behind. The camera follows the rocket as it climbs into the sky, transitioning to a wide shot showing the launch site near a coastal area with marshlands and the ocean in the background. The scene then cuts to a close-up of the rocket’s first stage engine firing during ascent, followed by a view of the second stage separating and deploying a payload. The payload, a large cylindrical spacecraft with a white thermal blanket, is shown deploying in space, with its structure unfolding and mechanical components becoming visible. The video then shifts to a NASA Mission Control Center, where Everett Bolduc, identified as a Flight Controller, speaks to the camera, gesturing as he explains the mission. The final shots return to space, showing the deployed spacecraft rotating and stabilizing in orbit, with Earth visible in the background. The National Geographic logo is consistently displayed in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

segments.caption: The video opens with an exterior view of a large, white, inflatable module attached to the International Space Station, its segmented thermal blanket glowing under direct sunlight against the blackness of space. The camera then cuts to a man in a dark suit and glasses, speaking in a control room filled with monitors, likely discussing the module’s deployment. The scene transitions to the interior of the module, showing a circular hatch with a black, ribbed interior and various instruments, followed by a close-up of the module’s exterior as it expands. An astronaut in a blue flight suit is seen maneuvering inside the module, adjusting equipment near yellow tanks and cables. The video then cuts to Jeff Hoffman, a former NASA astronaut and MIT professor, speaking in a dimly lit room with technical equipment in the background, identified by on-screen text. The perspective shifts to a view from inside the ISS’s Cupola, looking out at Earth’s curvature and the station’s truss structure. The camera pans across Earth’s surface, showing swirling clouds, blue oceans, and landmasses, then captures a stunning view of the aurora borealis glowing green over the planet’s limb. The final shots show Earth from orbit, with the Moon visible as a small, bright object in the dark sky, and a close-up of the ISS’s solar array against the backdrop of Earth’s atmosphere and clouds.

segments.caption: The video opens with a breathtaking satellite view of Earth’s coastline, transitioning to a close-up of an older man with white hair, identified as Jeff Hoffman, speaking in a dimly lit room with technical equipment in the background. The scene shifts to a stunning night view of Earth from space, showcasing the planet’s illuminated cities against the dark void, followed by a serene twilight sky with a bright celestial object—likely the Moon—visible above silhouetted tree branches. The narrative then moves inside the International Space Station, where astronauts are seen floating in microgravity, working with laptops and equipment; one astronaut, wearing a red shirt, is shown handling a document, while another in a blue shirt interacts with a control panel. A woman, identified as Dava Newman, appears in an interview setting, speaking directly to the camera. The ISS interior continues to be explored, showing a transparent module with green plants growing under artificial light, and a view from the Cupola window revealing Earth’s curvature and swirling clouds. The segment concludes with an exterior shot of the ISS orbiting Earth, followed by a dramatic rocket launch at dusk, with bright flames and smoke erupting from the launch pad as the rocket ascends into the sky. Throughout, the National Geographic logo remains in the bottom right corner.

The narrative then shifts to an interview with Don Pettit, an astronaut in a blue NASA flight suit, speaking inside a spacecraft or control room. A view from inside the ISS’s Cupola module shows Earth through the large circular window, followed by a spacewalk scene where an astronaut in a white spacesuit works on the station’s exterior, with Earth and the blackness of space in the background. The segment concludes with a breathtaking view of Earth’s curvature and aurora borealis from the ISS, with the station’s components visible in the foreground.
